content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 29, 2004

Jolie Houston
Berliner Cohen
Ten Almaden Boulevard, 11th Floor
San Jose, CA 95113-2233

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-083

Dear Ms. Houston:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of City of Gilroy Mayor Albert Pinheiro for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTION

Will Mayor Albert Pinheiro's position on the board of directors of Bonfante Gardens, Inc. disqualify him from decisions before the Gilroy City Council regarding Bonfante Gardens and its property?

CONCLUSION

No. Mayor Pinheiro is an unpaid director of Bonfante Gardens, Inc., a 501(c)(3) organization. He has no financial interest in Bonfante Gardens, Inc. and, thus, will have no conflict of interest in decisions regarding the Bonfante Gardens and its property.

_______________________

1 Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04-083
Page No. 2

FACTS

Bonfante Gardens, Inc., is a nonprofit Internal Revenue Code section 501(c)(3)
corporation organized as a public benefit corporation and, as such, holds a position for a
city official on its board of directors ("Board"). Bonfante Gardens currently operates
educational horticultural gardens and an amusement park for young children.

It is your understanding that Bonfante Gardens has stated that it will be applying
to the city for residential development ordinance units ("RDO") for a portion of its
property ("Property") pursuant to the city's ordinance, No. 2002-6. The RDO unit
allocation process is the city's growth control mechanism, and a developer must either be
allocated the RDO units by the city council, or qualify for an exemption for such units
prior to any residential development. You further understand that, after the city council
allocates RDO units to the Property, Bonfante Gardens is proposing to sell the Property
to a third party developer.

In order to actually proceed with development on the Property, the developer
must apply to the city for additional land use entitlements for the Property, such as a
general plan amendment, rezoning, tentative map and architectural and site review. It is
your understanding that Bonfante Gardens may continue to be the owner of the Property
during part or all of the entitlement process. The city council must approve those land
use applications.

ANALYSIS

Conflict of Interest

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.

A public official has a financial interest in a governmental decision within the
meaning of the Act if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests.
(Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard, eight-
step analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in
a given governmental decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Step 1 - Public official.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; regulation 18700(b)(1).) "Public official" is defined as "every
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . . ."

File No. A-04-083
Page No. 3

(Section 82048.) A "local government agency" means a county, city or district of any kind, including a school district, or any other local political subdivision or any county board or commission. (Section 82041.) As mayor for the City of Gilroy, Mr. Pinheiro is a "public official" for purposes of the Act (see sections 82041, 82048), and the conflict-of-interest rules apply to him.

Step 2 - Making, participating in making, or using an official position to influence governmental decisions.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official "make[s] [participa[s] in making or in any way attempt[s] to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." (Section 87100; regulation 18700(b)(2).) The Commission has adopted a series of regulations which define "making," "participating in making," and "influencing" a governmental decision, and which provide certain exceptions. (Regulations 18702.-18702.4.)

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position, commits his or her agency to any course of action, votes on a matter, obligates or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before any member, officer, employee, or official of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) Mayor Pinheiro will be making or participating and votes on decisions regarding Bonfante Gardens and its property.

Step 3 - Identifying the economic interests.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from economic interests as defined in regulations 18703-18703.5. Identifying which, if any, of these economic interests are held by a public official is the third step in analyzing under the Act. (See regulation 18700(b)(3).) There are six kinds of such economic interests:

■ A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a));

2 An "indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater." (Section 87103.)

File No. A-04-083
Page No. 4

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in
which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation
18703.1(b));

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in
which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more
(section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of income,
including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more
within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(c);
regulation 18703.3);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to
him or her if the gifts aggregate to $320 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision (section 87103(e); regulation 18703.4);

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or
her immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 18703.5).

Of the six economic interests, only two appear to be potentially implicated by
your facts. Those two categories are "source of income" (section 87103(c); regulation
18703.3) and acting as a director or officer of a business entity (section 87103(d);
regulation 18703.1(b)). With respect to source of income, you stated that Mayor Pinheiro
receives no compensation for his time on the board.
Additionally, with respect to being a
director or officer of a business entity, a nonprofit organization is not considered a
business entity under the Act. (Section 82005.) Mayor Pinheiro has no economic interest
in Bonfante Gardens, Inc. and, therefore no financial interest in decisions regarding
Bonfante Gardens before the city council.

Steps 4 – 8.

We have not gone on to describe the latter five steps because we have concluded
that Mayor Pinheiro does not have a financial interest in the decision. For your future
reference, we have enclosed a pamphlet entitled, "Can I Vote?" that explains the entire
eight-step process.

File No. A-04-083
Page No. 5

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Adrianne Korchmaros
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosure

sections.analysis:
Conflict of Interest

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.

A public official has a financial interest in a governmental decision within the meaning of the Act if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard, eight-step analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Step 1 - Public official.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections 87100, 87103; regulation 18700(b)(1).) "Public official" is defined as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . ."

(Section 82048.) A "local government agency" means a county, city or district of any kind, including a school district, or any other local political subdivision or any county board or commission. (Section 82041.) As mayor for the City of Gilroy, Mr. Pinheiro is a "public official" for purposes of the Act (see sections 82041, 82048), and the conflict-of-interest rules apply to him.

Step 2 - Making, participating in making, or using an official position to influence governmental decisions.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official "make[s], participate[s] in making or in any way attempt[s] to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." (Section 87100; regulation 18700(b)(2).) The Commission has adopted a series of regulations which define "making," "participating in making," and "influencing" a governmental decision, and which provide certain exceptions. (Regulations 18702-18702.4.)

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) Mayor Pinheiro will be making or participating in making governmental decisions if he participates and votes on decisions regarding Bonfante Gardens and its property.

Step 3 - Identifying the economic interests.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from economic interests as defined in regulations 18703-18703.5. Identifying which, if any, of these economic interests are held by a public official is the third step in analyzing a potential conflict of interest under the Act. (See regulation 18700(b)(3).) There are six kinds of such economic interests:

▪ A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment2 of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a));

2 An "indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater." (Section 87103.)

▪ A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));

▪ A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2);

▪ A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3);

▪ A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $320 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(e); regulation 18703.4);

▪ A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is known as the “personal financial effects” rule (section 87103; regulation 18703.5).

Of the six economic interests, only two appear to be potentially implicated by your facts. Those two categories are “source of income” (section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3) and serving as a director or officer of a business entity (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b)). With respect to source of income, you stated that Mayor Pinheiro receives no compensation for his time on the board. Additionally, with respect to being a director or officer of a business entity, a nonprofit organization is not considered a business entity under the Act. (Section 82005.) Mayor Pinheiro has no economic interest in Bonfante Gardens, Inc. and, therefore no financial interest in decisions regarding Bonfante Gardens before the city council.

Steps 4 – 8.

We have not gone on to describe the latter five steps because we have concluded that Mayor Pinheiro does not have a financial interest in the decision. For your future reference, we have enclosed a pamphlet entitled, “Can I Vote?” that explains the entire eight-step process.
